Dr. Aboli Borse · MBBS · Dubai / Mumbai

Medicine taught me to care for one patient at a time. My work is about the systems that care for everyone at once.

Quality and operations at a JCI-accredited hospital in Dubai. Before that: rural primary care in Maharashtra, and years of teaching and community-building with WHO India, UNESCO's bioethics network, and MSAI.

See the work Get in touch

The route so far

Where the work has taken me

Selected work

Case files

Care and systems
CASE 01

Quality improvement, end to end

Setting JCI/DHA-accredited hospitals · India and DubaiMethod Clinical and process audits · KPI dashboards · risk and incident reviewRole Quality and patient safety

Quality work asks one question: did the system behave as designed, and what should change when it didn't? I do it with the people who run the system every day: nurses, physicians, allied health, admin, IT. Understand where the workflow strains, design changes they can sustain, and run the loop: measure, structure, intervene, re-measure.

Clinical audits, from question to intervention
Full-cycle audits designed against explicit standards, from proposal through re-audit. Recent work includes:
  • Perioperative pathway audits on timeliness, documentation completeness and referral flow, surfacing the gap between clinical execution and standardised process
  • Treatment and eradication compliance audits in gastroenterology, closed with re-audit cycles
  • Appropriateness audits at proposal stage for high-volume diagnostic procedures
Every audit starts as a testable question, not a check-box: what should the system do, what does it do, and where is the gap concentrated?
Quality improvement projects and workflow redesign
Where audits reveal recurring patterns, QIPs turn them into interventions. Current work spans discharge summary workflow redesign, pairing timeliness and completeness as co-primary KPIs, and neurovascular assessment compliance in orthopaedic post-operative care, structured as a twelve-month clinical audit and improvement cycle. Interventions map to specific drivers with named ownership, so the improvement holds after the project closes.
Risk assessment and FMEA
Failure Mode and Effects Analysis on new clinical technologies and workflows, most recently for AI-assisted radiology software across four modules. Every identified failure mode traces to the manufacturer's own Instructions for Use, producing an audit-defensible risk register reviewable by JCI and DHA assessors.
Incident review, CAPA and KPI dashboards
Incident reports reviewed with root-cause analysis. Corrective and preventive actions tracked to closure with recurrence monitoring. Quality dashboards and KPI reports prepared for management review across fully digital workflows. The everyday scaffolding that makes safer care a measured outcome rather than an aspiration.

The through-line. Quality improvement is designed with the clinical team, not imposed on it. My work shortens the loop, sharpens the instrumentation, and moves what patients actually experience: shorter waits, safer handoffs, decisions made on time.

CASE 02

Primary care where the system thins out

Setting Rural and tribal Maharashtra · SMBT Charitable Hospital and linked PHCsMethod Clinical practice · community outreach · school and camp programmesRole Clinical intern, community and primary care focus

A rural hospital teaches you what healthcare looks like without infrastructure. Long referral pathways, uncertain follow-up, wide variation in health literacy. The outcome depends on what happens outside the consulting room as much as inside it. A year here shaped how I think about every system since.

Clinical practice across departments
A year-long rotating internship across Community Medicine, Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Paediatrics, Medicine, Surgery and Emergency Medicine. Antenatal, intrapartum and postnatal care. Outpatient and inpatient management under supervision. Minor procedures and continuity of care within multidisciplinary teams.
Community outreach and camps
Twelve community health sessions in rural and tribal areas on maternal health, infectious disease prevention and nutrition, coordinated with ASHAs and ANMs. Ten mobile medical camps and seven school health programmes, contributing to screenings, immunisations and primary care for 800+ beneficiaries.

What it taught me. Interventions that ignore the last mile don't hold. Health outcomes are a systems problem long before they reach a KPI dashboard. The design instinct I bring to hospital quality started here.

Education and community
CASE 03

Bioethics education, rebuilt as a skill you can practise

Setting UNESCO Bioethics Unit, MSAI · college → nationalMethod Curriculum redesign · experiential formats · programme evaluationRole Unit Member → General Secretary → Steering Committee

I teach. Medical school taught me clinical medicine well and taught almost nothing about what earns a patient's trust: ethical reasoning, communication, medico-legal awareness, professional conduct. Clinicians discover these gaps at the bedside, after graduation. I have spent four years closing them earlier, for students and nurses, first at my college and then nationally under UDBHR.

The Moot Court on Medical Law and Bioethics
The centrepiece format. Students argue real medico-legal grey zones (consent disputes, confidentiality conflicts, communication breakdowns) from both sides. Founded at my own college after making the case to the Dean, then scaled through the UNESCO Bioethics Unit into a national intercollege competition. Now in its fourth edition under new leadership. Participants stop asking what is right and start asking how do we decide, and by which principles, which is the actual clinical skill.
Redesigning the national bioethics programme
As General Secretary of the UNESCO Bioethics Unit, I led a 15-member team curating national activity-based campaigns aligned with UDBHR. The unit refreshed the format calendar with case-based discussion and interactive campaigns, drafted policy statements, and evaluated 46+ on-ground events reaching 3,000+ medical students. The design brief was consistent: replace lectures with experiences that make principles felt, not filed.
Peer-education content on communication and cultural sensitivity
Beyond ethics-as-subject, the work extended to the adjacent skills clinical training under-teaches: patient communication, breaking bad news, cultural sensitivity in care. National peer-to-peer education modules on these topics were designed for medical students and nurses alike. These are workforce-readiness skills, not specialist ones.
Governance continuity, from Steering Committee
Currently on the unit's Steering Committee, providing policy guidance to successor General Secretaries. This ensures the educational objectives outlast individual terms and the formats keep evolving on evidence rather than habit.

Outcome. A body of teaching work that treats ethics, communication, medico-legal awareness and professional conduct as skills you can practise. Designed to run without its designers, iterated across cohorts, and governed so the learning discipline outlasts individual terms.

CASE 04

Standing up an AMR awareness unit with WHO India

Setting MSAI × WHO Country Office, IndiaMethod Programme design · partnership managementRole Vice-President, Internal Affairs

Antimicrobial resistance starts with everyday prescribing culture, which makes 20,000 future prescribers the right audience. With the WHO Country Office in India, we built a dedicated AMR unit inside MSAI to turn awareness into practice.

Intervention: the campaign portfolio
The unit ran on-ground national AMR workshops alongside a full campaign calendar (World AMR Awareness Week, Global Handwashing Day, national webinars, Project GoBLUE), pairing institutional credibility from WHO with the reach of a peer network.
  • Four offline national workshops for direct student engagement
  • Campaign programming aligned to WHO's global AMR calendar
  • A multi-year AMR strategy formulated so successor teams inherit a roadmap rather than a restart
On-ground activities during my term
The scope of MSAI's on-ground work extended well beyond AMR. I helped shape and support a wider portfolio across regions and standing committees:
  • National General Assembly, Nashik: vice-chair of the organising committee, paired with a peer-to-peer training programme for 350 students
  • Regional conferences in Hyderabad, Sikkim and Amritsar, plus a national research conference in Surat
  • IFMSA International General Assembly, New Delhi: 500 delegates, 56 countries
  • Bioethics campaigns under UDBHR: 46+ on-ground events reaching 3,000+ students
  • International exchange programme: hosted 23 international students at my institution end-to-end
  • Full activity record published in MSAI's previous activities archive and the VANI annual publication

Outcome. What began as a single conference workshop evolved into a fully operational, WHO-collaborative unit with a strategy running to 2027.

CASE 05

100+ hours of on-ground training, delivered nationwide

Setting National peer-education programme, IndiaMethod Curriculum design · facilitationRole IFMSA-certified national trainer

Peer education is only worth doing if it changes what learners can do afterwards. As an IFMSA-certified national trainer, I designed and delivered interactive modules on communication, ethics and leadership: 100+ hours of on-ground training reaching 250+ people. The metric that matters is how many left able to teach.

Intervention: scaling the format
Training programmes ran across national and regional events throughout the country, from small workshop rooms to conference floors, including a peer-to-peer programme for 350 students alongside the Annual General Meeting in Nashik. Modules were deliberately experiential: role-play, case discussion and structured feedback in place of lectures, so participants left able to run the sessions themselves.

Outcome. The measure of peer education is how many can now teach, and how the next cohort's questions have shifted. Participants left able to run the sessions themselves. The sessions run without me now, which is the point.

CASE 06

Coordinating across 56 countries: assemblies and exchanges

Setting IFMSA global meetings · International exchange programmeMethod Comparative medical education · exchange design · cross-cultural coordinationRole Executive Board, MSAI

Medical education looks entirely different from a Brazilian federal university, a European teaching hospital, and an Indian medical school. Knowing what to keep, cut and borrow in your own system requires seeing the others. My international work has been that: studying medical education through every lens available, and building exchange that leaves participants changed.

Intervention: the international portfolio
A body of work sustained across the term, held together by the same brief. Understand medical education from every angle, and make exchange meaningful for the students on both sides.
  • IFMSA International General Assembly, New Delhi: co-organised for 500 delegates from 56 countries, joint responsibility for logistics, protocol and delegate engagement
  • Successive IFMSA global meetings: MSAI delegations engaging in policy sessions, activities fairs and exchange negotiations, including a co-proposed policy on plastics in healthcare adopted internationally
  • Bilateral exchange programme: hosted 23 international students at my institution end-to-end, from academic placements to cultural programming, so the visit produced real clinical and educational exposure
  • Full activity record in the MSAI archive and VANI annual

Outcome. A working understanding of medical education viewed from many national systems at once, and a habit of designing exchange as an actual learning intervention. The students who came through those programmes came away with something they could take into their own careers.

About

The same question kept following me

During my internship year in rural Maharashtra, I watched skilled clinicians produce very different outcomes for very similar patients. The difference was rarely knowledge. It was the system around them: whether investigations came back in time, whether the referral went through, whether anyone followed up. I found I couldn't stop noticing this, in every hospital I worked in afterwards.

So I followed the question. Into quality and operations roles in Mumbai and Dubai, where the gap between good clinicians and good outcomes gets measured against JCI and DHA standards. And into education, where the same gap shows up earlier: students taught medicine thoroughly and taught almost nothing about communication, ethics, or how to keep learning. The hospital work and the teaching work are the same work to me. Both are about building systems that let capable people do their best consistently.

Off the chart

Master Scuba Diver100+ logged dives. Thirty metres down, with a finite air supply, you learn the most useful skill in any hospital: staying calm is a decision, not a feeling.
Kathak VisharadEight years of classical training. The long discipline of getting fundamentals exactly right.
Horse riderPartnership with something that has its own opinions. Useful preparation for stakeholders.

How I work with people

Most of what I've built was built by a team

The numbers on this site (24 national officers with an extended team of 72, a 15-member unit, 350-participant sessions, 500-delegate assemblies) are teams I worked inside. Culture is what holds a team together when the founder leaves the room. I've spent more time designing that than anything else.

I try to hear before I speak

Ethical dilemmas are built on acknowledging multiple truths. So is a good team. I make a conscious effort to hear every perspective in the room before I share mine. The strongest decisions come from people feeling actually heard.

Ownership over control

The programmes I designed early on suffered from me stepping in too much. I'd offer input that quietly reduced others' sense of ownership. I've since learned that leadership means creating the space for others to interpret and own the vision themselves.

Culture is engineered, not assumed

Feedback rhythms, transition rituals, review systems, room for open disagreement without hierarchy penalising it. These are the boring infrastructure of team culture, and I've tried to design them as deliberately as any curriculum or audit.

Continuity is the highest form of leadership

On the MSAI Supervisory Council I helped introduce periodic audit and review systems for board members. Accountability, feedback and transition planning designed to outlive any single term. A team that keeps working after you leave is the only real test.

Ethical dilemmas are built on acknowledging multiple truths. So is effective leadership. Shared purpose, not control, is what makes a team hold.

The auditor, audited

If I audit hospitals, it's only fair I audit myself

Same methodology I use professionally. Explicit standards, honest measurement, findings, corrective action. Results below are self-reported and, unlike my hospital audits, entirely unverified.

Auditee Dr. A. BorseScope Professional conduct, 2021 to presentStandard Self-imposed, unreasonably high
Projects that still run after I leave
100%
Finding: moot court in 4th edition, AMR unit on a 2027 roadmap. Conformity maintained.
Audits closed with re-audit, not just a report
92%
Finding: one re-audit pending. Auditee aware. Auditee slightly defensive.
Meetings ended on time
74%
Finding: overruns correlate strongly with the phrase "one last thing about the workflow."
Saying no to interesting new projects
41%
Finding: major non-conformity. Recurring since 2021. See corrective action below.
Asking for help before it becomes urgent
63%
Finding: improving year on year. Root cause identified as "I've almost got it."
ConformingNon-conformityTarget
Corrective and preventive actionAuditee has been advised to take on fewer projects. Auditee has responded by adding "build a portfolio site" to the project list. CAPA remains open. Recurrence probable.

Connecting the dots

One question, seen from five sides

Clinical audits, community engagement, ethics teaching, national campaigns, learning experiments. These have never felt like separate interests to me. Read across the columns. The same pattern runs through each.

the working question

Why do capable people, given the right information, still act in ways that produce inconsistent outcomes? And what does the system around them need to look like to change that?

↓↓↓↓↓
01 · Student behaviour

Behaviour shifts when relevance is felt

Where I met itWatching moot court participants argue a real medico-legal case. They engaged with a principle they had been able to recite for years but never used.
The design question is always: what would make this feel real to the person you're trying to reach?
02 · Community engagement

What stays alive after you leave is what you built

Where I met itRunning a 20,000-member national organisation. Enthusiasm doesn't sustain it. The boring infrastructure does: peer ownership, review systems, formats that survive board transitions.
Community is measured in continuity, not in headcount.
03 · Ethics

Ambiguity is a skill you can practise

Where I met itTeaching ethics through argumentation and case discussion. The students who learned to defend a position under pressure were the same ones who made better clinical calls.
The formats that build ethical reasoning build clinical judgement in the same motion.
04 · Quality improvement

An audit is a structured way of noticing drift

Where I met itPerioperative audits, discharge QIPs, AI risk registers. The tools are ordinary. The discipline of measuring against an explicit standard is what turns "usual practice" back into designed practice.
The audit loop and the learning loop are the same loop.
05 · Campaigns and experiments

Formats are experiments, kept or cut on evidence

Where I met itRunning AMR campaigns with WHO India across workshops, on-ground programmes and webinars. Each format tested for engagement rather than shipped on hope.
Reaching people at scale is a product problem: ship, measure, iterate.
the pattern beneath

Every column is the same design instinct. Engineer the system around the person, then measure honestly whether it worked.

Open questions

Questions I haven't answered yet

Most portfolios show you what someone knows. These are the four questions my work keeps circling without resolving, and where my thinking currently stands on each.

Q.01

Why do checklists transform aviation but only nudge hospitals?

Where I've got to: aviation standardised the crew before it standardised the checklist. Hospitals often try the reverse, handing a tool to a hierarchy that hasn't agreed to be interrupted by it. The instrument is rarely the problem. The permission structure around it is.

Q.02

What is the audit equivalent for learning?

We measure attendance and satisfaction, and neither predicts what a learner can do a month later. My working answer: audit learning the way you audit a clinical pathway. Define the observable behaviour, measure it at a distance from the teaching, and treat the gap as a system finding, not a student failing.

Q.03

Can culture be measured without flattening it?

Retention, surveys and engagement scores all lag reality. The most honest instrument I've found so far is behavioural: how a team acts in its worst week, when the founder is absent, and whether disagreement travels upward safely. I don't yet know how to turn that into a number worth trusting.

Q.04

Why does improvement so often leave with the improver?

The pattern I keep seeing: sustainability is treated as a closing task instead of a day-one deliverable. My working hypothesis, tested across a moot court, an AMR unit and several audits: ownership transfer has to be designed into the start, or it never happens at the end.

If you have seen a real answer to any of these, or think one of mine is wrong, that is the single best reason to email me.
